title: Flow Algorithms: How Much Power Can Actually Get Through
learningObjectives: Explain the difference between finding the shortest path and finding the maximum flow through a network
Explain what a transmission line's capacity constraint means for a flow network model
Implement or apply a max-flow algorithm to determine the maximum deliverable power between two points
Identify the min-cut (the bottleneck) that limits a network's maximum flow, and explain the max-flow min-cut relationship
whyItMatters: Dijkstra's algorithm found the lowest-loss path, but it silently assumed transmission lines have unlimited capacity — real lines have a maximum safe carrying capacity, and knowing the cheapest route doesn't tell you whether enough power can actually get through it during peak demand.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's capacity-aware routing calculation — answering not just 'what's the cheapest path' but 'how much power can actually reach this substation given real line capacity limits,' the exact gap Dijkstra's algorithm alone leaves open.

problem.summary: The cheapest route might not have enough capacity to carry actual demand.
problem.content: Your minimum-loss route from the last lesson might pass through a transmission line rated for only 3000 kW, but the actual demand you need to route through it is 5000 kW — the cheapest path isn't useful if it can't carry enough power.

motivation.summary: Max flow asks a fundamentally different question than shortest path.
motivation.content: This is a fundamentally different question from shortest path: not 'what's the cheapest way to send power' but 'what's the most power that can possibly get from A to B given every line's capacity limit' — exactly the question that matters when demand approaches a network's real limits.

theory.summary: Max-flow finds the greatest deliverable flow; max-flow min-cut identifies the true bottleneck.
theory.content: A flow network assigns each edge a capacity — the maximum amount that can flow through it — and the max-flow problem asks for the greatest total flow achievable from a source to a sink without exceeding any edge's capacity. The Ford-Fulkerson approach (and refinements like Edmonds-Karp) repeatedly finds an 'augmenting path' with spare capacity from source to sink and pushes more flow along it, until no augmenting path remains. The max-flow min-cut theorem states that the maximum achievable flow exactly equals the capacity of the network's min-cut — the smallest total capacity of edges that, if removed, would disconnect the source from the sink — meaning the min-cut identifies the actual bottleneck limiting the whole network's throughput.

visualExplanation.summary: Water-pipe-style flow through capacity-labeled edges, converging on the highlighted min-cut bottleneck.
visualExplanation.content: A flow network with capacity labels on each edge shows water-pipe-style flow volume animated through the network, converging on a highlighted min-cut set of edges that represents the network's true bottleneck.

interactiveDemo.summary: Adjust edge capacities and watch max-flow and the min-cut edges update live.
interactiveDemo.content: Adjust individual edge capacities on a live flow network and watch the computed max-flow value and the highlighted min-cut edges update accordingly, directly observing which edge is currently the bottleneck.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: compute max-flow on a capacity-constrained network.
guidedCoding.content:
```python
import networkx as nx

G = nx.DiGraph()
G.add_edge("main-feed", "substation-1", capacity=4000)
G.add_edge("substation-1", "substation-4", capacity=3000)
G.add_edge("main-feed", "substation-2", capacity=3000)
G.add_edge("substation-2", "substation-4", capacity=2500)

max_flow_value, flow_dict = nx.maximum_flow(G, "main-feed", "substation-4")
print(max_flow_value)
```

independentCoding.summary: Model realistic capacities and identify the min-cut for a specific substation.
independentCoding.content: Model your grid topology as a capacity-constrained flow network with realistic line capacities, compute the maximum deliverable power to a specific substation, and identify the min-cut edges limiting it.

debugging.summary: Fix a bidirectional line modeled with mismatched directed-edge capacities.
debugging.content: A bidirectional transmission line was modeled as two separate directed edges with mismatched capacities in each direction, producing an unrealistic max-flow result. Identify the modeling error and fix the capacity assignment.

reflection.summary: The min-cut tells you where infrastructure investment matters most.
reflection.content: Once you know the min-cut for a critical substation, what does that actually tell you about where infrastructure investment would have the biggest impact on how much power can reach it?

teachBack.summary: Explain the max-flow min-cut relationship using your own topology's bottleneck.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone the max-flow min-cut relationship using your own grid topology's computed bottleneck as the concrete example.

miniProject.summary: Report capacity-constrained substations and their min-cut bottlenecks under peak demand.
miniProject.content: Compute the maximum deliverable power to each substation in your grid under realistic capacity constraints, identify which substations are capacity-constrained during peak demand (per your Mission 3/4 forecasts), and report the specific min-cut bottleneck for each.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This connects grid capacity analysis back to Mission 3/4's demand predictions.
portfolioEvidence.content: This capacity-aware analysis directly answers whether the grid can actually deliver what the forecasting models predict will be needed — connecting this skill folder's work back to Mission 3 and 4's demand predictions.

labs.title: Max-Flow Capacity
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise modeling a capacity-constrained grid as a flow network and computing max-flow/min-cut, with a planted mismatched-bidirectional-capacity bug to fix.

simulations.title: Max-Flow Min-Cut Visualizer
simulations.description: Live flow network with adjustable capacities, showing computed max-flow value and highlighted min-cut bottleneck edges.

flashcards.front: What does the max-flow min-cut theorem state?
flashcards.back: The maximum achievable flow through a network exactly equals the total capacity of the network's min-cut — the smallest set of edges that, if removed, would disconnect the source from the sink.

flashcards.front: Why is a modeling mistake likely if a bidirectional transmission line's two directed edges have different capacities?
flashcards.back: A physical bidirectional line typically has the same carrying capacity in either direction; mismatched capacities usually indicate an error in how the line was translated into the directed-edge flow model, not a real asymmetry.

rubric: Correctly identifies and fixes the mismatched bidirectional-capacity modeling error
Computed max-flow and min-cut are correctly interpreted in terms of real infrastructure bottlenecks
Capacity-constrained substations are correctly cross-referenced against Mission 3/4 demand forecasts
